title: Stripping a drop-in chair seat to the frame
subtitle: Remove the cover, padding, and webbing; leave a clean frame
excerpt: How to strip a drop-in chair seat back to the bare frame: removing the top cover, calico under-cover, hessian, padding, and old webbing layer by layer, leaving a clean timber frame ready for re-upholstering.
sourceNotes: Traditional upholstery stripping practice. City & Guilds Upholstery Level 2 syllabus. David James A Complete Course in Upholstery (2014). Spratt's Practical Upholstery (1929).

content.text: Stripping a drop-in seat layer by layer shows its construction and reveals what needs replacing. A seat that sags may only need new webbing; one that feels dead may need to be stripped to bare wood and rebuilt entirely. Stripping carefully preserves the frame and lets you assess each component before deciding what to reuse.
Step 1, remove any gimp braid or trim.

If the seat has a decorative trim along the edges, ease it free first. Trim glued with contact adhesive comes off cleanly when a flat tool is worked along the line. Trim fixed with gimp pins needs each pin prised out before the braid will lift.

Turn the seat upside down. Work the staple remover under each staple or tack in turn and lever it free. Go around all four sides before pulling the fabric away; pulling against staples still in the frame tears the fabric. If the cover might be useful as a cutting template, fold it flat and set aside.
layer is stapled or tacked in the same way as the top cover. Remove all fixings and lift it away. Calico in good condition can be kept as a cutting template for the replacement.

Foam padding lifts straight off if it was contact-glued to the seat board or hessian. If it has bonded firmly over years of use, slide the utility knife blade flat between the foam and the surface beneath. Hair or fibre stuffing in older seats should be discarded entirely; it cannot be reconditioned and must be replaced with new material.
layer covers the webbing. Remove all its fixings and lift it away. On modern foam-and-board drop-in seats there may be no hessian layer; the foam is glued directly to the board.

Remove every staple, tack, and strip of old webbing. Knock any remaining staple heads flush or below the rail surface with the tack hammer; raised metal tears the new webbing and prevents it sitting flat. The frame is now bare and ready for inspection.

With the frame stripped, press each corner joint. Any movement means the joint should be re-glued before re-upholstering. Check the rail top surfaces for deep tack pitting; rails heavily pitted from previous re-upholstery cycles may need a thin tacking strip pinned to the rail face to give fresh wood for the staples.
Staples left partially in the frame.
A staple with one leg still proud of the surface tears the new webbing. Use the tack hammer to knock every partial staple fully home or pull it out completely before proceeding.
